payload.answer: Store each (id, name, value) triplet as a separate memory card with explicit relationships. Handle duplicates via timestamped resolution or aggregation. Preserve ambiguity with flags and avoid superseded facts.
citations: Rule: Atomic Granularity for Repeated Triplets

entity_resolution.reason: No pre-existing entity matches the (id, name, value) triplet structure in durable memory. Requires atomic extraction.

memory_cards.statement: For a CSV table with 500 rows containing repeated (id, name, value) triplets, store each unique (id, name, value) combination as a separate memory card to preserve granularity and avoid ambiguity.

If duplicate (id, name, value) triplets exist, retain only the most recent timestamped entry (if available) or merge into a single card with aggregated metadata (e.g., 'count: 3') to avoid redundancy.
Never store superseded or deleted facts as current. Mark superseded cards with a 'status: superseded' tag and a 'replaced_by' relationship to the newer card.
Preserve ambiguity in relationships where (id, name) pairs map to multiple values. Represent as a 'one-to-many' relationship with a 'disambiguation_needed' flag.

details: No superseded facts committed as current.

repair_options: Merge duplicate cards into a single card with aggregated metadata if no timestamp exists.
Add a 'disambiguation_required' tag to cards with conflicting (id, name) → value mappings.
Archive superseded cards with a 'status: superseded' tag and link to the replacement.
